page: Vultr vx1-m-96c-768g-5760s is an x86_64 architecture server instance within the VX1 Cloud Compute family. It features 96 vCPUs powered by AMD processors, configured with a shared CPU allocation model. The system is equipped with 768.0 GB of system memory, which yields a ratio of 8.0 GB of RAM per vCPU. Local storage is provided via a bundled 5760 GB NVMe SSD, and the instance includes one complimentary public IPv4 address. No dedicated GPU hardware is present on this instance.

The primary architectural tradeoff of this instance is its shared CPU allocation despite its large scale of 96 vCPUs. While the high memory capacity of 768.0 GB and the substantial 5760 GB NVMe SSD storage provide resources suitable for data-dense workloads, the shared CPU model means compute performance may be subject to resource contention from other tenants on the host. This configuration offers qualitative cost efficiency by providing a massive memory footprint and large local NVMe storage capacity at a lower price point than dedicated-core equivalents, making it a viable option when memory capacity and storage space are prioritized over guaranteed, sustained single-core CPU performance.

This server configuration is suited for memory-intensive workloads that do not require continuous, guaranteed CPU performance. Appropriate use cases include large-scale caching layers, read-heavy database replicas, in-memory data processing, and staging environments for data analytics pipelines where storage capacity and memory density are the primary bottlenecks.
description: Vultr vx1-m-96c-768g-5760s is an x86_64 server instance in the VX1 Cloud Compute family, featuring 96 shared AMD vCPUs, 768.0 GB of RAM, and 5760 GB of local NVMe SSD storage. With an 8.0 GB memory-to-core ratio, this instance is designed for memory-intensive and storage-heavy workloads. The shared CPU allocation presents a clear tradeoff, as compute resources are shared with other tenants, which may lead to variable CPU performance. However, this model provides high qualitative cost efficiency for organizations requiring massive memory and storage capacities without the premium cost of dedicated CPU cores. It is suited for large-memory caching, read-heavy databases, and non-production data processing pipelines.
